text: Which aggregation stage is equivalent to a SQL WHERE clause?
explanation: $match filters documents, just like WHERE in SQL. Always place $match as early as possible in the pipeline to reduce the number of documents processed by later stages.
sqlEquivalent: WHERE clause

text: In $group, what does _id represent?
explanation: In $group, _id is the grouping key — equivalent to GROUP BY in SQL. Set _id to null to aggregate all documents into one group.
sqlEquivalent: GROUP BY clause — the _id is what you're grouping by

text: Which accumulator counts the number of documents in each group?
explanation: { $sum: 1 } adds 1 for each document in the group, effectively counting. There's also a $count stage for counting total documents, but within $group you use $sum: 1.
hint: Add 1 for every document in the group.
sqlEquivalent: COUNT(*)

text: What does $unwind do to an array field?
options: Removes the array from the document
Flattens the array — creates one document per array element
correctAnswer: Flattens the array — creates one document per array element
explanation: $unwind deconstructs an array field. A document with genres: ['fiction', 'classic'] becomes two documents — one with genres: 'fiction' and one with genres: 'classic'.
sqlEquivalent: UNNEST() or LATERAL JOIN to expand an array

text: What SQL clause does $lookup most closely resemble?
explanation: $lookup joins documents from another collection, similar to a LEFT JOIN in SQL. It matches documents from the 'from' collection where localField = foreignField.
hint: It combines data from two collections.
sqlEquivalent: LEFT JOIN another_table ON local_field = foreign_field

text: Complete this pipeline to count books grouped by authorName:
explanation: $group groups documents by _id (here: authorName) and applies accumulators. $sum: 1 counts documents in each group.
hint: The stage that groups and aggregates.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT author_name, COUNT(*) FROM books GROUP BY author_name

text: Which stage reshapes documents — keeping/excluding fields and adding computed ones?
explanation: $project controls which fields appear in the output and can add computed fields using expressions. 1 = include, 0 = exclude.
hint: Like the SELECT list in SQL.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT col1, col2, col1 * 2 AS double_col FROM ...

text: Which stage limits the number of documents in the pipeline output?
explanation: $limit restricts the pipeline to the first N documents. Used with $sort to get top-N results.
sqlEquivalent: LIMIT N

text: What accumulator computes the average of a field across grouped documents?
explanation: $avg: '$price' computes the arithmetic mean of all price values in the group.
sqlEquivalent: AVG(price)

text: In what order are aggregation pipeline stages executed?
options: Left to right — each stage processes output from the previous stage
$match and $sort always run first regardless of position
correctAnswer: Left to right — each stage processes output from the previous stage
explanation: Pipeline stages run left to right (top to bottom in vertical form). Each stage's output is the input for the next. Order matters greatly for both correctness and performance.
hint: Like a Unix pipe: cmd1 | cmd2 | cmd3

text: Complete this pipeline: find the total revenue (sum of totalAmount) grouped by order status:
explanation: _id: '$status' groups by the status field. $sum: '$totalAmount' sums the totalAmount field in each group. Field references in aggregation use $ prefix.
hint: Field references in aggregation use the $ prefix.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT status, SUM(total_amount) FROM orders GROUP BY status

text: You want HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 in MongoDB aggregation. Which pipeline sequence implements this?
explanation: Use $match AFTER $group to filter on grouped values. $match before $group = WHERE; $match after $group = HAVING.
sqlEquivalent: HAVING COUNT(*) > 2

text: Complete the $lookup to join books with authors on books.authorId = authors._id:
explanation: from: collection to join. localField: field in the current collection. foreignField: field in the joined collection. as: name of the output array field.
hint: from = the other collection, localField = your key, foreignField = their key.
sqlEquivalent: LEFT JOIN authors ON books.author_id = authors._id

text: After a $lookup, the joined data is stored as an array. How do you turn it into a single object (for a 1:1 join)?
explanation: $lookup always returns an array (even for 1:1 joins). $unwind flattens it to a single document. This effectively turns a LEFT JOIN result into an INNER JOIN (documents without a match are dropped).
sqlEquivalent: The difference between LEFT JOIN (keeps nulls) and INNER JOIN (drops non-matches)

text: How do you create a computed field 'priceWithTax' as price * 1.1 in $project?
explanation: $multiply takes an array of values and multiplies them. Field references need the $ prefix. Other math operators: $add, $subtract, $divide.
hint: Use $multiply with an array of operands.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT price * 1.1 AS price_with_tax FROM books

text: Write an aggregation pipeline to find the top 3 authors by number of books in the collection, showing authorName and bookCount.
explanation: 1. $group by authorName, count books. 2. $sort by count descending. 3. $limit to 3. 4. $project to rename _id to authorName.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT author_name, COUNT(*) FROM books GROUP BY author_name ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 3

text: A book has genres: ['fiction', 'classic']. After $unwind: '$genres', how many documents does this one book produce?
explanation: $unwind creates one document per array element. { title: '1984', genres: ['fiction', 'classic'] } becomes two documents: { title: '1984', genres: 'fiction' } and { title: '1984', genres: 'classic' }.

text: Translate: SELECT status, COUNT(*) as cnt, AVG(total_amount) as avg_order FROM orders GROUP BY status HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ORDER BY avg_order DESC;
explanation: $group first, then $match (HAVING), then $sort. Note: MongoDB field names use camelCase (totalAmount not total_amount).
sqlEquivalent: GROUP BY + HAVING + ORDER BY

text: Why should $match appear as early as possible in an aggregation pipeline?
options: $match can only be used at the start
Early $match reduces the document count for all subsequent stages, improving performance
correctAnswer: Early $match reduces the document count for all subsequent stages, improving performance
explanation: Every document that passes $match must be processed by all subsequent stages. An early $match eliminates irrelevant documents before expensive operations like $lookup or $group.

text: After $lookup, what happens to books that have no matching author in the authors collection?
options: Those books are excluded from results
authorInfo is set to an empty array []
correctAnswer: authorInfo is set to an empty array []
explanation: $lookup is like a LEFT JOIN — it includes all documents from the left (source) collection. If no match is found in the joined collection, the output array is empty. $unwind after $lookup with no match would drop the document (like INNER JOIN).
hint: $lookup behaves like LEFT JOIN — no match = empty array.

text: Write an aggregation pipeline to find the average book price per genre. Genres is an array — you need to unwind it first.
explanation: $unwind explodes the genres array into separate documents, then $group aggregates by genre.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT genre, AVG(price) FROM books JOIN book_genres ON ... GROUP BY genre

text: In $project, how do you add a computed field that concatenates 'title' and 'authorName' as 'titleByAuthor'?
explanation: $concat takes an array of strings (or field references) and concatenates them. Literal strings are not prefixed with $.
hint: $concat takes an array of values to join.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT title || ' by ' || author_name AS title_by_author

text: What is a 'pipeline' $lookup (the advanced form)?
options: A $lookup where the join condition is a custom aggregation pipeline, not just field equality
correctAnswer: A $lookup where the join condition is a custom aggregation pipeline, not just field equality
explanation: Advanced $lookup uses 'let' and 'pipeline' instead of localField/foreignField. It allows complex join conditions, filtering the joined collection with $match/$project inside the pipeline.
hint: Instead of field equality, you write a pipeline for the join.

text: What is the $facet stage used for?
options: Running multiple aggregation pipelines on the same input in parallel, returning results in a single document
correctAnswer: Running multiple aggregation pipelines on the same input in parallel, returning results in a single document
explanation: $facet allows multiple parallel sub-pipelines in one aggregation. Each sub-pipeline is named and runs on the same input documents. Returns all results in one document. Great for search result facets (count by category + count by price range + top results).
hint: Multiple pipelines running on the same data at once.

text: Collect all book titles grouped by authorName into an array field 'titles':
explanation: $push accumulates values into an array within a group. Unlike $addToSet (which deduplicates), $push keeps all values including duplicates.
hint: Push each title into the array.
sqlEquivalent: STRING_AGG or ARRAY_AGG in PostgreSQL

text: You have books with authorId referencing authors. Write a pipeline to get each author with their average book rating. What order of stages is correct?
options: Match authors → $lookup books from books collection → $unwind books → $group by author
Start from books: $group by authorId with $avg rating → $lookup authors for names
Start from books: $lookup authors → $unwind → $group by authorName with $avg rating
correctAnswer: Start from books: $group by authorId with $avg rating → $lookup authors for names
explanation: It's more efficient to group first (reducing document count), then $lookup author names. This avoids expanding all books to get all author info first.

text: Translate: SELECT title, price, CASE WHEN price < 12 THEN 'budget' WHEN price < 16 THEN 'standard' ELSE 'premium' END AS tier FROM books;
options: db.books.aggregate([{ $project: { title: 1, price: 1, tier: { $switch: { branches: [{ case: { $lt: ['$price', 12] }, then: 'budget' }, { case: { $lt: ['$price', 16] }, then: 'standard' }], default: 'premium' } } } }])
correctAnswer: db.books.aggregate([{ $project: { title: 1, price: 1, tier: { $switch: { branches: [{ case: { $lt: ['$price', 12] }, then: 'budget' }, { case: { $lt: ['$price', 16] }, then: 'standard' }], default: 'premium' } } } }])
explanation: $switch is MongoDB's equivalent of SQL CASE WHEN. branches is an array of { case, then } pairs. default is the else clause.
sqlEquivalent: CASE WHEN price < 12 THEN 'budget' WHEN price < 16 THEN 'standard' ELSE 'premium' END

text: What is the $out stage and why should it be used carefully?
options: Writes pipeline results to a new/existing collection, replacing it entirely
correctAnswer: Writes pipeline results to a new/existing collection, replacing it entirely
explanation: $out writes the pipeline results to a specified collection. If the collection already exists, it is REPLACED entirely. This is irreversible — use with caution.
hint: $out writes to a collection and replaces it.

text: What does $addToSet do in $group, and how is it different from $push?
options: Same as $push but sorts the result
Adds elements to an array, deduplicating — no duplicates in the output
Adds only the first occurrence of each value
correctAnswer: Adds elements to an array, deduplicating — no duplicates in the output
explanation: $addToSet builds an array of unique values (like a Set). $push adds all values including duplicates. Use $addToSet when you want distinct values.
sqlEquivalent: ARRAY_AGG(DISTINCT value) in PostgreSQL

text: Write an aggregation to get each order's customer email, total order amount, and the number of items in the order.
explanation: $size: '$items' counts the elements of the items array. $project includes the fields we need.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT customer_email, total_amount, array_length(items, 1) FROM orders
